reasoning:
Country-level: The vehicles have long, white EU license plates with a blue band and black text plus two circular seals in the center—consistent with German license plates. Road markings (thick, white lane arrows and dashed lane lines), the small traffic island bollard style, and the signal heads on curved poles also match German urban road furniture. Language cues include multiple “HOTEL” signs and a short street- or place-name fragment ending in “-BERG,” a common German toponymic suffix. These cues point strongly to Germany and rule out neighboring countries with different plate formats (e.g., Netherlands’ yellow plates or France’s different signal hardware).
City-level: The architecture (prewar mid-rise blocks with mansard roofs), dense street grid, and abundant mature trees resemble cities in North Rhine–Westphalia or the Ruhr (Duisburg/Essen/Mülheim) more than southern cities like Munich (more stucco/Bavarian details) or Hamburg (more red-brick warehouses near water). Direction signs on the left appear to be the German tourist/wayfinding stack typical in NRW cities. The visible “...BERG” placename strongly suggests “Kaiserberg,” a well-known district/park/zoo area in Duisburg, strengthening a Ruhr-area inference and making Cologne/Düsseldorf less likely.
Street-level: A corner hotel with a red vertical “HOTEL” blade sign and a matching red awning over the entrance sits on the right, immediately before a right-turn slip into a side street. The main carriageway has three lanes with straight/turn arrows, a small pedestrian refuge with a blue-topped bollard, and a curved traffic-signal mast at the junction ahead. A stacked white direction sign cluster stands on the left verge. This specific combination—corner hotel signage, nearby refuge bollard, stacked wayfinding sign, and the probable “...BERG” wall plaque—helps distinguish this junction from other nearby streets and is consistent with a road approaching the Kaiserberg area in Duisburg.
Overall, the scene most plausibly fits Duisburg, NRW, Germany, likely near the Kaiserberg locality. Confidence is limited by unreadable text, but multiple German-specific cues align.
